<div id='hep-ex'></div>

# hep-ex [[Back]](#toc)

### [477] [Retrieval-Augmented Question Answering over Scientific Literature for the Electron-Ion Collider](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.02259)
*Tina. J. Jat,T. Ghosh,Karthik Suresh*

Main category: hep-ex

TL;DR: The paper describes a locally-deployed Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) system for answering domain-specific questions on the Electron-Ion Collider experiment using arXiv articles and an open-source language model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a cost-effective and privacy-preserving Q&A application that addresses domain-specific questions in experimental nuclear physics.

Method: A RAG system was developed using a locally-indexed database of arXiv articles related to the Electron-Ion Collider experiment and an open-source LLaMA model for generating answers.

Result: The RAG system provides a resource-constrained solution for domain-specific Q&A, maintaining data privacy and avoiding exposure of sensitive pre-publication data.

Conclusion: The system is an effective, private, and cost-efficient solution for domain-specific question answering in experimental nuclear physics, with plans for future expansion to include more data sources and improved orchestration.

Abstract: To harness the power of Language Models in answering domain specific specialized technical questions, Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) is been used widely. In this work, we have developed a Q\&A application inspired by the Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG), which is comprised of an in-house database indexed on the arXiv articles related to the Electron-Ion Collider (EIC) experiment - one of the largest international scientific collaboration and incorporated an open-source LLaMA model for answer generation. This is an extension to it's proceeding application built on proprietary model and Cloud-hosted external knowledge-base for the EIC experiment. This locally-deployed RAG-system offers a cost-effective, resource-constraint alternative solution to build a RAG-assisted Q\&A application on answering domain-specific queries in the field of experimental nuclear physics. This set-up facilitates data-privacy, avoids sending any pre-publication scientific data and information to public domain. Future improvement will expand the knowledge base to encompass heterogeneous EIC-related publications and reports and upgrade the application pipeline orchestration to the LangGraph framework.

</details>


<div id='econ.GN'></div>

# econ.GN [[Back]](#toc)

### [478] [From Automation to Augmentation: A Framework for Designing Human-Centric Work Environments in Society 5.0](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01364)
*Cristian Espinal Maya*

Main category: econ.GN

TL;DR: The paper tackles the lack of measurable and practical methods to evaluate Society 5.0 and human-centric Industry 5.0 concepts by developing an operational model that includes workplace design and human-AI interaction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address gaps in human-AI complementarity and workplace design's impact on augmentation outcomes, which are essential for achieving economically optimal human-centric technology integration.

Method: The researchers develop a novel model where the augmentation function depends on a five-dimensional workplace design. They conduct a systematic review of 120 papers, analyze secondary empirical data from a large manufacturing survey, and design a new diagnostic instrument called the Workplace Augmentation Design Index (WADI).

Result: The findings establish that human-centric workplace design is profit-maximizing when cognitive capital exceeds a threshold, management practices amplify technology returns, and decision authority allocation and task orchestration are critical dimensions for effective human-AI complementarity.

Conclusion: Human-centric workplace design is economically optimal under certain conditions and measurable through the newly proposed WADI instrument, providing actionable insights for Society 5.0 transitions.

Abstract: Society 5.0 and Industry 5.0 call for human-centric technology integration, yet the concept lacks an operational definition that can be measured, optimized, or evaluated at the firm level. This paper addresses three gaps. First, existing models of human-AI complementarity treat the augmentation function phi(D) as exogenous -- dependent only on the stock of AI deployed -- ignoring that two firms with identical technology investments achieve radically different augmentation outcomes depending on how the workplace is organized around the human-AI interaction. Second, no multi-dimensional instrument exists linking workplace design choices to augmentation productivity. Third, the Society 5.0 literature proposes human-centricity as a normative aspiration but provides no formal criterion for when it is economically optimal. We make four contributions. (1) We endogenize the augmentation function as phi(D, W), where W is a five-dimensional workplace design vector -- AI interface design, decision authority allocation, task orchestration, learning loop architecture, and psychosocial work environment -- and prove that human-centric design is profit-maximizing when the workforce's augmentable cognitive capital exceeds a critical threshold. (2) We conduct a PRISMA-guided systematic review of 120 papers (screened from 6,096 records) to map the evidence base for each dimension. (3) We provide secondary empirical evidence from Colombia's EDIT manufacturing survey (N=6,799 firms) showing that management practice quality amplifies the return to technology investment (interaction coefficient 0.304, p<0.01). (4) We propose the Workplace Augmentation Design Index (WADI), a 36-item theory-grounded instrument for diagnosing human-centricity at the firm level. Decision authority allocation emerges as the binding constraint for Society 5.0 transitions, and task orchestration as the most under-researched dimension

</details>


<div id='math.CA'></div>

# math.CA [[Back]](#toc)

### [479] [A Determinantal Approach to a Sharp $\ell^1-\ell^\infty-\ell^2$ Norm Inequality](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01525)
*Jose Antonio Lara Benitez*

Main category: math.CA

TL;DR: The paper provides a linear-algebraic proof of a specific inequality involving three fundamental norms in finite-dimensional spaces and proves the optimality of the constant used.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to provide a compact proof of a known inequality relating three common norms (\( \|x\|_1 \), \( \|x\|_\infty \), and \( \|x\|_2 \)) on finite-dimensional spaces, which has various applications in optimization and numerical analysis.

Method: The authors use the determinantal structure of a family of quadratic forms to derive the inequality and prove the optimality of the constant \((1+\sqrt{p})/2\).

Result: The inequality \( \|x\|_1\,\|x\|_\infty \le \frac{1+\sqrt{p}}{2}\,\|x\|_2^2 \) was proven for all \( x \in \mathbb{R}^p \), and the constant \((1+\sqrt{p})/2\) was shown to be optimal.

Conclusion: The paper successfully provides a short proof of the inequality and establishes its validity and optimality, contributing useful insights into norm relationships in finite-dimensional spaces.

Abstract: We give a short linear--algebraic proof of the inequality \[ \|x\|_1\,\|x\|_\infty \le \frac{1+\sqrt{p}}{2}\,\|x\|_2^2, \] valid for every \(x\in\mathbb{R}^p\). This inequality relates three fundamental norms on finite-dimensional spaces and has applications in optimization and numerical analysis. Our proof exploits the determinantal structure of a parametrized family of quadratic forms, and we show the constant $(1+\sqrt{p})/2$ is optimal.

</details>


<div id='math.ST'></div>

# math.ST [[Back]](#toc)

### [480] [Observable Geometry of Singular Statistical Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01267)
*Sean Plummer*

Main category: math.ST

TL;DR: This paper introduces a parameterization-invariant framework called observable charts to analyze the intrinsic statistical structure of singular models, addressing issues of non-identifiability and extending classical asymptotic theory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Singular models exhibit non-identifiability and classical theory breaks down. Existing methods are heavily dependent on parameterization, obscuring intrinsic statistical properties.

Method: Develop a framework using observable charts—functionals of data distributions—to create local coordinate systems independent of parameterization. Formalize metrics like observable completeness and observable order to study statistical distinguishability.

Result: Demonstrates observable order as a bound on divergence rates in singular models and applies it to examples like reduced-rank regression and Gaussian mixtures, highlighting identifiable structures and singularities.

Conclusion: Observable charts offer a unified framework to study singular models, bridging intrinsic geometric structure and statistical distinguishability while extending to singular settings.

Abstract: Singular statistical models arise whenever different parameter values induce the same distribution, leading to non-identifiability and a breakdown of classical asymptotic theory. While existing approaches analyze these phenomena in parameter space, the resulting descriptions depend heavily on parameterization and obscure the intrinsic statistical structure of the model. In this paper, we introduce an invariant framework based on \emph{observable charts}: collections of functionals of the data distribution that distinguish probability measures. These charts define local coordinate systems directly on the model space, independent of parameterization. We formalize \emph{observable completeness} as the ability of such charts to detect identifiable directions, and introduce \emph{observable order} to quantify higher-order distinguishability along analytic perturbations. Our main result establishes that, under mild regularity conditions, observable order provides a lower bound on the rate at which Kullback-Leibler divergence vanishes along analytic paths. This connects intrinsic geometric structure in model space to statistical distinguishability and recovers classical behavior in regular models while extending naturally to singular settings. We illustrate the framework in reduced-rank regression and Gaussian mixture models, where observable coordinates reveal both identifiable structure and singular degeneracies. These results suggest that observable charts provide a unified and parameterization-invariant language for studying singular models and offer a pathway toward intrinsic formulations of invariants such as learning coefficients.

</details>


<div id='eess.IV'></div>

# eess.IV [[Back]](#toc)

### [481] [OkanNet: A Lightweight Deep Learning Architecture for Classification of Brain Tumor from MRI Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01264)
*Okan Uçar,Murat Kurt*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: The paper proposes and compares two deep learning methods for automatic brain tumor detection using MRI images, showing a trade-off between accuracy and computational efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MRI imaging manual analysis is time-consuming and error-prone; automating the process could reduce human error and improve efficiency in detecting and classifying brain tumors.

Method: Two methods were applied: a custom low-cost CNN architecture 'OkanNet', and Transfer Learning using a pre-trained ResNet-50 model on the ImageNet dataset.

Result: ResNet-50 achieved superior performance with 96.49% accuracy, while OkanNet was faster (311s training time) and better suited for systems with limited computational resources.

Conclusion: ResNet-50 is preferred for high accuracy needs, while OkanNet fits scenarios requiring computational efficiency and faster processing, demonstrating a balance between depth and efficiency.

Abstract: Medical imaging techniques, especially Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI), are accepted as the gold standard in the diagnosis and treatment planning of neurological diseases. However, the manual analysis of MRI images is a time-consuming process for radiologists and is prone to human error due to fatigue. In this study, two different Deep Learning approaches were developed and analyzed comparatively for the automatic detection and classification of brain tumors (Glioma, Meningioma, Pituitary, and No Tumor). In the first approach, a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture named "OkanNet", which has a low computational cost and fast training time, was designed from scratch. In the second approach, the Transfer Learning method was applied using the 50-layer ResNet-50 [1] architecture, pre-trained on the ImageNet dataset. In experiments conducted on an extended dataset compiled by Masoud Nickparvar containing a total of $7,023$ MRI images, the Transfer Learning-based ResNet-50 model exhibited superior classification performance, achieving $96.49\%$ Accuracy and $0.963$ Precision. In contrast, the custom OkanNet architecture reached an accuracy rate of $88.10\%$; however, it proved to be a strong alternative for mobile and embedded systems with limited computational power by yielding results approximately $3.2$ times faster ($311$ seconds) than ResNet-50 in terms of training time. This study demonstrates the trade-off between model depth and computational efficiency in medical image analysis through experimental data.

</details>


### [482] [DenOiS: Dual-Domain Denoising of Observation and Solution in Ultrasound Image Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.02105)
*Can Deniz Bezek,Orcun Goksel*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: This paper introduces DenOiS, a framework to enhance image reconstruction by addressing noise and inaccuracies in measurement and imaging models. It combines observation refinement and diffusion-based plug-and-play reconstruction for robust results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to overcome challenges in medical imaging, such as noise-sensitive regularization, inexact imaging models, and the dependency on measurement quality that limit reconstruction accuracy.

Method: The authors propose DenOiS, which integrates a dual approach: observation refinement to correct measurements and a diffusion-based plug-and-play (PnP) reconstruction to enhance robustness under data imperfections.

Result: Experiments demonstrate that DenOiS improves image reconstruction quality in speed-of-sound imaging by successfully compensating for noisy observations and simplified imaging models.

Conclusion: DenOiS achieves high-fidelity image reconstruction even when trained on simulation data, highlighting its potential for generalization to real-world scenarios and addressing limitations in existing methods.

Abstract: Medical imaging aims to recover underlying tissue properties, using inexact (simplified/linearized) imaging models and often from inaccurate and incomplete measurements. Analytical reconstruction methods rely on hand-crafted regularization, sensitive to noise assumptions and parameter tuning. Among deep learning alternatives, plug-and-play (PnP) approaches learn regularization while incorporating imaging physics during inference, outperforming purely data-driven methods. The performance of all these approaches, however, still strongly depends on measurement quality and imaging model accuracy. In this work, we propose DenOiS, a framework that denoises both input observations and resulting solution in their respective domains. It consists of an observation refinement strategy that corrects degraded measurements while compensating for imaging model simplifications, and a diffusion-based PnP reconstruction approach that remains robust under missing measurements. DenOiS enables generalization to real data from training only in simulations, resulting in high-fidelity image reconstruction with noisy observations and inexact imaging models. We demonstrate this for speed-of-sound imaging as a challenging setting of quantitative ultrasound image reconstruction.

</details>


<div id='cs.CR'></div>

# cs.CR [[Back]](#toc)

### [483] [EXHIB: A Benchmark for Realistic and Diverse Evaluation of Function Similarity in the Wild](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01554)
*Yiming Fan,Jun Yeon Won,Ding Zhu,Melih Sirlanci,Mahdi Khalili,Carter Yagemann*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper focuses on Binary Function Similarity Detection (BFSD), introducing a benchmark (EXHIB) that spans five datasets to uncover generalization issues in existing models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the lack of a comprehensive benchmark for comparing BFSD models effectively, as existing datasets do not reflect the diversity of real-world applications.

Method: The authors created EXHIB, a benchmark with five realistic datasets. They evaluated nine models across various paradigms using EXHIB to test generalization performance.

Result: Performance degradations of up to 30% were observed on firmware and semantic datasets, indicating generalization gaps to high-level semantic differences.

Conclusion: Current BFSD models are not robust to diverse real-world transformations, especially regarding high-level semantic differences, which calls for improved evaluation practices.

Abstract: Binary Function Similarity Detection (BFSD) is a core problem in software security, supporting tasks such as vulnerability analysis, malware classification, and patch provenance. In the past few decades, numerous models and tools have been developed for this application; however, due to the lack of a comprehensive universal benchmark in this field, researchers have struggled to compare different models effectively. Existing datasets are limited in scope, often focusing on a narrow set of transformations or types of binaries, and fail to reflect the full diversity of real-world applications.
  We introduce EXHIB, a benchmark comprising five realistic datasets collected from the wild, each highlighting a distinct aspect of the BFSD problem space. We evaluate 9 representative models spanning multiple BFSD paradigms on EXHIB and observe performance degradations of up to 30% on firmware and semantic datasets compared to standard settings, revealing substantial generalization gaps. Our results show that robustness to low- and mid-level binary variations does not generalize to high-level semantic differences, underscoring a critical blind spot in current BFSD evaluation practices.

</details>


### [484] [From Component Manipulation to System Compromise: Understanding and Detecting Malicious MCP Servers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01905)
*Yiheng Huang,Zhijia Zhao,Bihuan Chen,Susheng Wu,Zhuotong Zhou,Yiheng Cao,Xin Hu,Xin Peng*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper addresses security challenges in MCP, presents a component-centric dataset for malicious MCP servers, and introduces Connor, a behavior-based detection framework achieving high effectiveness in identifying attacks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MCP simplifies LLM integration with external tools but introduces overlooked attack surface vulnerabilities, especially multi-component ones.

Method: Researchers created a dataset of MCP server attacks and developed Connor, a two-stage detection system analyzing component behavior before and during execution.

Result: Connor achieved a 94.6% F1-score, outperforming existing methods by up to 59.6%, and successfully identified malicious servers in real-world scenarios.

Conclusion: Component-centric analysis enhances MCP security, and Connor provides an effective detection framework against complex attacks.

Abstract: The model context protocol (MCP) standardizes how LLMs connect to external tools and data sources, enabling faster integration but introducing new attack vectors. Despite the growing adoption of MCP, existing MCP security studies classify attacks by their observable effects, obscuring how attacks behave across different MCP server components and overlooking multi-component attack chains. Meanwhile, existing defenses are less effective when facing multi-component attacks or previously unknown malicious behaviors.
  This work presents a component-centric perspective for understanding and detecting malicious MCP servers. First, we build the first component-centric PoC dataset of 114 malicious MCP servers where attacks are achieved as manipulation over MCP components and their compositions. We evaluate these attacks' effectiveness across two MCP hosts and five LLMs, and uncover that (1) component position shapes attack success rate; and (2) multi-component compositions often outperform single-component attacks by distributing malicious logic. Second, we propose and implement Connor, a two-stage behavioral deviation detector for malicious MCP servers. It first performs pre-execution analysis to detect malicious shell commands and extract each tool's function intent, and then conducts step-wise in-execution analysis to trace each tool's behavioral trajectories and detect deviations from its function intent. Evaluation on our curated dataset indicates that Connor achieves an F1-score of 94.6%, outperforming the state of the art by 8.9% to 59.6%. In real-world detection, Connor identifies two malicious servers.

</details>


### [485] [RuleForge: Automated Generation and Validation for Web Vulnerability Detection at Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01977)
*Ayush Garg,Sophia Hager,Jacob Montiel,Aditya Tiwari,Michael Gentile,Zach Reavis,David Magnotti,Wayne Fullen*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: RuleForge, an AWS system, automatically creates JSON-based detection rules for new vulnerabilities from structured Nuclei templates, deploying LLM-based validation to optimize sensitivity and specificity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The skyrocketing number of CVEs in recent years has overwhelmed security teams, driving the need for automated detection rule generation to keep pace with emerging threats.

Method: RuleForge leverages Nuclei YAML templates for structured CVE data and employs LLMs as judges to validate the sensitivity and specificity of candidate detection rules, integrating a feedback loop for iterative improvement.

Result: RuleForge achieved an AUROC of 0.75, reduced false positives by 67%, and demonstrated the feasibility of generating detection rules from both structured and unstructured data sources.

Conclusion: LLMs can enhance cybersecurity automation with appropriate safeguards such as feedback loops, human-in-the-loop validation, and careful management of overconfidence issues in rule generation.

Abstract: Security teams face a challenge: the volume of newly disclosed Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVEs) far exceeds the capacity to manually develop detection mechanisms. In 2025, the National Vulnerability Database published over 48,000 new vulnerabilities, motivating the need for automation. We present RuleForge, an AWS internal system that automatically generates detection rules--JSON-based patterns that identify malicious HTTP requests exploiting specific vulnerabilities--from structured Nuclei templates describing CVE details. Nuclei templates provide standardized, YAML-based vulnerability descriptions that serve as the structured input for our rule generation process.
  This paper focuses on RuleForge's architecture and operational deployment for CVE-related threat detection, with particular emphasis on our novel LLM-as-a-judge (Large Language Model as judge) confidence validation system and systematic feedback integration mechanism. This validation approach evaluates candidate rules across two dimensions--sensitivity (avoiding false negatives) and specificity (avoiding false positives)--achieving AUROC of 0.75 and reducing false positives by 67% compared to synthetic-test-only validation in production. Our 5x5 generation strategy (five parallel candidates with up to five refinement attempts each) combined with continuous feedback loops enables systematic quality improvement. We also present extensions enabling rule generation from unstructured data sources and demonstrate a proof-of-concept agentic workflow for multi-event-type detection. Our lessons learned highlight critical considerations for applying LLMs to cybersecurity tasks, including overconfidence mitigation and the importance of domain expertise in both prompt design and quality review of generated rules through human-in-the-loop validation.

</details>


### [486] [Safety, Security, and Cognitive Risks in World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01346)
*Manoj Parmar*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: World models enhance autonomous agent decision-making but create safety, security, and cognitive risks due to vulnerabilities in training, rollout errors, and adversarial attacks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore risks associated with the widespread adoption of world models in AI and offer solutions to mitigate these challenges.

Method: The authors provide formal definitions of trajectory persistence and representational risk, taxonomy of attacker capabilities, a unified threat model, empirical adversarial attack experiments, and risk illustrations across deployment scenarios.

Result: Empirical tests confirm adversarial attack amplification and reduction in system performance. Risks are demonstrated in real-world deployment contexts, highlighting vulnerabilities.

Conclusion: World models should be treated as safety-critical assets, demanding robust adversarial hardening, alignment techniques, and human-centered governance frameworks.

Abstract: World models -- learned internal simulators of environment dynamics -- are rapidly becoming foundational to autonomous decision-making in robotics, autonomous vehicles, and agentic AI. Yet this predictive power introduces a distinctive set of safety, security, and cognitive risks. Adversaries can corrupt training data, poison latent representations, and exploit compounding rollout errors to cause catastrophic failures in safety-critical deployments. World model-equipped agents are more capable of goal misgeneralisation, deceptive alignment, and reward hacking precisely because they can simulate the consequences of their own actions. Authoritative world model predictions further foster automation bias and miscalibrated human trust that operators lack the tools to audit.
  This paper surveys the world model landscape; introduces formal definitions of trajectory persistence and representational risk; presents a five-profile attacker capability taxonomy; and develops a unified threat model extending MITRE ATLAS and the OWASP LLM Top 10 to the world model stack. We provide an empirical proof-of-concept on trajectory-persistent adversarial attacks (GRU-RSSM: A_1 = 2.26x amplification, -59.5% reduction under adversarial fine-tuning; stochastic RSSM proxy: A_1 = 0.65x; DreamerV3 checkpoint: non-zero action drift confirmed). We illustrate risks through four deployment scenarios and propose interdisciplinary mitigations spanning adversarial hardening, alignment engineering, NIST AI RMF and EU AI Act governance, and human-factors design. We argue that world models must be treated as safety-critical infrastructure requiring the same rigour as flight-control software or medical devices.

</details>


### [487] [SelfGrader: Stable Jailbreak Detection for Large Language Models using Token-Level Logits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01473)
*Zikai Zhang,Rui Hu,Olivera Kotevska,Jiahao Xu*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: SelfGrader is a lightweight method using token-level logits to detect jailbreak attacks on large language models (LLMs), offering reliable performance and efficiency over existing guardrails.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address vulnerabilities of LLMs to jailbreak attacks and limitations of existing guardrail methods that struggle with latency or text generation randomness.

Method: A numerical grading system using token-level logits, where queries are graded based on logit distribution. A dual-perspective scoring rule aligns scores with human perception of maliciousness, reducing false positives.

Result: SelfGrader reduces attack success rate (ASR) by up to 22.66% on LLaMA-3-8B while providing significantly lower memory (up to 173x) and latency overhead (up to 26x) than existing methods.

Conclusion: SelfGrader offers an efficient and interpretable solution for detecting malicious queries, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines with better stability and lower resource usage.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are powerful tools for answering user queries, yet they remain highly vulnerable to jailbreak attacks. Existing guardrail methods typically rely on internal features or textual responses to detect malicious queries, which either introduce substantial latency or suffer from the randomness in text generation. To overcome these limitations, we propose SelfGrader, a lightweight guardrail method that formulates jailbreak detection as a numerical grading problem using token-level logits. Specifically, SelfGrader evaluates the safety of a user query within a compact set of numerical tokens (NTs) (e.g., 0-9) and interprets their logit distribution as an internal safety signal. To align these signals with human intuition of maliciousness, SelfGrader introduces a dual-perspective scoring rule that considers both the maliciousness and benignness of the query, yielding a stable and interpretable score that reflects harmfulness and reduces the false positive rate simultaneously. Extensive experiments across diverse jailbreak benchmarks, multiple LLMs, and state-of-the-art guardrail baselines demonstrate that SelfGrader achieves up to a 22.66% reduction in ASR on LLaMA-3-8B, while maintaining significantly lower memory overhead (up to 173x) and latency (up to 26x).

</details>


### [488] [RefinementEngine: Automating Intent-to-Device Filtering Policy Deployment under Network Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01627)
*Davide Colaiacomo,Chiara Bonfanti,Cataldo Basile*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces RefinementEngine, a tool that automates the translation of high-level security intents into deployable network configurations, validated via real-world cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Security operations require manually translating high-level security intents into deployable rules, which is slow, error-prone, and prone to misconfigurations, particularly in complex networks.

Method: RefinementEngine uses network topology, device-specific security details, high-level intents, and Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) reports to automatically generate deployable configurations.

Result: RefinementEngine correctness and applicability were demonstrated through real-world use cases involving packet and web filtering policies derived from CTI reports.

Conclusion: RefinementEngine simplifies security intent translation and adapts to new data, addressing key operational challenges in security operations centers (SOCs).

Abstract: Translating security intent into deployable network enforcement rules and maintaining their effectiveness despite evolving cyber threats remains a largely manual process in most Security Operations Centers (SOCs). In large and heterogeneous networks, this challenge is complicated by topology-dependent reachability constraints and device-specific security control capabilities, making the process slow, error-prone, and a recurring source of misconfigurations. This paper presents RefinementEngine, an engine that automates the refinement of high-level security intents into low-level, deployment-ready configurations. Given a network topology, devices, and available security controls, along with high-level intents and Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) reports, RefinementEngine automatically generates settings that implement the desired intent, counter reported threats, and can be directly deployed on target security controls. The proposed approach is validated through real-world use cases on packet and web filtering policies derived from actual CTI reports, demonstrating both correctness, practical applicability, and adaptability to new data.

</details>


### [489] [AEGIS: Adversarial Entropy-Guided Immune System -- Thermodynamic State Space Models for Zero-Day Network Evasion Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.02149)
*Vickson Ferrel*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: AEGIS introduces a novel, physics-based defense mechanism using hyperbolic models to counter adversarial attacks in encrypted traffic analysis, achieving state-of-the-art performance metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional encrypted traffic analysis faces challenges due to vulnerabilities in Euclidean Transformer-based classifiers and growing adversarial morphing techniques.

Method: AEGIS leverages a 6-dimensional continuous-time flow physics approach projected into a Poincare manifold to detect anomalies, utilizing liquid time-constants and thermodynamic variance for analyzing packet flows.

Result: Evaluated on diverse adversarial traffic data, AEGIS achieved an F1-score of 0.9952, a True Positive Rate of 99.50%, and sub-millisecond latency for real-time inference, outperforming prior models.

Conclusion: AEGIS redefines encrypted traffic analysis by transitioning from byte payloads to continuous-time flow physics, proving highly effective against advanced adversarial attacks.

Abstract: As TLS 1.3 encryption limits traditional Deep Packet Inspection (DPI), the security community has pivoted to Euclidean Transformer-based classifiers (e.g., ET-BERT) for encrypted traffic analysis. However, these models remain vulnerable to byte-level adversarial morphing -- recent pre-padding attacks reduced ET-BERT accuracy to 25.68%, while VLESS Reality bypasses certificate-based detection entirely. We introduce AEGIS: an Adversarial Entropy-Guided Immune System powered by a Thermodynamic Variance-Guided Hyperbolic Liquid State Space Model (TVD-HL-SSM). Rather than competing in the Euclidean payload-reading domain, AEGIS discards payload bytes in favor of 6-dimensional continuous-time flow physics projected into a non-Euclidean Poincare manifold. Liquid Time-Constants measure microsecond IAT decay, and a Thermodynamic Variance Detector computes sequence-wide Shannon Entropy to expose automated C2 tunnel anomalies. A pure C++ eBPF Harvester with zero-copy IPC bypasses the Python GIL, enabling a linear-time O(N) Mamba-3 core to process 64,000-packet swarms at line-rate. Evaluated on a 400GB, 4-tier adversarial corpus spanning backbone traffic, IoT botnets, zero-days, and proprietary VLESS Reality tunnels, AEGIS achieves an F1-score of 0.9952 and 99.50% True Positive Rate at 262 us inference latency on an RTX 4090, establishing a new state-of-the-art for physics-based adversarial network defense.

</details>


### [490] [Seclens: Role-specific Evaluation of LLM's for security vulnerablity detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01637)
*Subho Halder,Siddharth Saxena,Kashinath Kadaba Shrish,Thiyagarajan M*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces SecLens-R, a multi-stakeholder evaluation framework for LLM-based vulnerability detection, addressing limitations of single-metric assessments by considering varied stakeholder priorities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks using a single metric cannot capture the diverse requirements of stakeholders like CISOs, engineering leaders, or AI officers in evaluating LLMs for vulnerability detection.

Method: The authors propose SecLens-R, a framework with 35 dimensions in 7 categories and 5 role-specific weighting profiles. Models are scored on a scale of 0-100 based on tasks across programming languages and vulnerability categories.

Result: Evaluation of 12 models across 406 tasks revealed significant score variability depending on stakeholder profiles, illustrating distinct trade-offs. For example, Qwen3-Coder scored highly for engineering leaders but poorly for CISOs.

Conclusion: Vulnerability detection in LLMs involves multi-objective trade-offs, and this framework provides nuanced, role-specific insights that single-metric evaluations cannot capture.

Abstract: Existing benchmarks for LLM-based vulnerability detection compress model performance into a single metric, which fails to reflect the distinct priorities of different stakeholders. For example, a CISO may emphasize high recall of critical vulnerabilities, an engineering leader may prioritize minimizing false positives, and an AI officer may balance capability against cost. To address this limitation, we introduce SecLens-R, a multi-stakeholder evaluation framework structured around 35 shared dimensions grouped into 7 measurement categories. The framework defines five role-specific weighting profiles: CISO, Chief AI Officer, Security Researcher, Head of Engineering, and AI-as-Actor. Each profile selects 12 to 16 dimensions with weights summing to 80, yielding a composite Decision Score between 0 and 100.
  We apply SecLens-R to evaluate 12 frontier models on a dataset of 406 tasks derived from 93 open-source projects, covering 10 programming languages and 8 OWASP-aligned vulnerability categories. Evaluations are conducted across two settings: Code-in-Prompt (CIP) and Tool-Use (TU). Results show substantial variation across stakeholder perspectives, with Decision Scores differing by as much as 31 points for the same model. For instance, Qwen3-Coder achieves an A (76.3) under the Head of Engineering profile but a D (45.2) under the CISO profile, while GPT-5.4 shows a similar disparity. These findings demonstrate that vulnerability detection is inherently a multi-objective problem and that stakeholder-aware evaluation provides insights that single aggregated metrics obscure.

</details>


### [491] [Combating Data Laundering in LLM Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01904)
*Muxing Li,Zesheng Ye,Sharon Li,Feng Liu*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces a method called synthesis data reversion (SDR) to counteract the effects of data laundering in large language models (LLMs), allowing data rights owners to better detect unauthorized training even when their proprietary data has been stylistically transformed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address how data rights owners can detect unauthorized use of their proprietary data in LLM training, especially under the challenges posed by data laundering, which obfuscates data provenance by stylistically transforming the information.

Method: The authors propose SDR, a method that abstracts laundering transformations into high-level goals and concrete details, then iteratively refines synthesized queries using an auxiliary LLM to better match suspected laundered data. This process helps reveal stronger detection signals in the target LLM.

Result: The SDR method was evaluated on the MIMIR benchmark and showed consistent improvements in detecting data misuse across various laundering techniques and LLM families, including Pythia, Llama2, and Falcon.

Conclusion: SDR serves as a practical and effective tool for data rights owners to counter data laundering, enhancing the detection of unauthorized data usage in diverse and obfuscated scenarios.

Abstract: Data rights owners can detect unauthorized data use in large language model (LLM) training by querying with proprietary samples. Often, superior performance (e.g., higher confidence or lower loss) on a sample relative to the untrained data implies it was part of the training corpus, as LLMs tend to perform better on data they have seen during training. However, this detection becomes fragile under data laundering, a practice of transforming the stylistic form of proprietary data, while preserving critical information to obfuscate data provenance. When an LLM is trained exclusively on such laundered variants, it no longer performs better on originals, erasing the signals that standard detections rely on. We counter this by inferring the unknown laundering transformation from black-box access to the target LLM and, via an auxiliary LLM, synthesizing queries that mimic the laundered data, even if rights owners have only the originals. As the search space of finding true laundering transformations is infinite, we abstract such a process into a high-level transformation goal (e.g., "lyrical rewriting") and concrete details (e.g., "with vivid imagery"), and introduce synthesis data reversion (SDR) that instantiates this abstraction. SDR first identifies the most probable goal for synthesis to narrow the search; it then iteratively refines details so that synthesized queries gradually elicit stronger detection signals from the target LLM. Evaluated on the MIMIR benchmark against diverse laundering practices and target LLM families (Pythia, Llama2, and Falcon), SDR consistently strengthens data misuse detection, providing a practical countermeasure to data laundering.

</details>


### [492] [APEX: Agent Payment Execution with Policy for Autonomous Agent API Access](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.02023)
*Mohd Safwan Uddin,Mohammed Mouzam,Mohammed Imran,Syed Badar Uddin Faizan*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: APEX adapts the HTTP 402 protocol for payment gating to fiat workflows like UPI, ensuring secure, policy-controlled monetization without reliance on cryptocurrencies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous agents increasingly need robust monetization and payment mechanisms, particularly aligned to fiat systems like UPI, as cryptocurrency-focused approaches often clash with regulatory frameworks.

Method: APEX implements a challenge-settle-consume lifecycle incorporating HMAC-signed tokens and idempotent settlement alongside FastAPI and SQLite for inspectable and reproducible operations.

Result: Findings show 27.3% reduced spending with policy enforcement, 52.8% success rate for valid requests, and security mechanisms achieving 100% block rate against replay attacks and invalid tokens, with low latency overhead.

Conclusion: APEX offers an adaptable payment infrastructure for fiat systems, balancing security, spend control, and reproducibility, making it suitable for agent-driven monetization needs.

Abstract: Autonomous agents are moving beyond simple retrieval tasks to become economic actors that invoke APIs, sequence workflows, and make real-time decisions. As this shift accelerates, API providers need request-level monetization with programmatic spend governance. The HTTP 402 protocol addresses this by treating payment as a first-class protocol event, but most implementations rely on cryptocurrency rails. In many deployment contexts, especially countries with strong real-time fiat systems like UPI, this assumption is misaligned with regulatory and infrastructure realities. We present APEX, an implementation-complete research system that adapts HTTP 402-style payment gating to UPI-like fiat workflows while preserving policy-governed spend control, tokenized access verification, and replay resistance. We implement a challenge-settle-consume lifecycle with HMAC-signed short-lived tokens, idempotent settlement handling, and policy-aware payment approval. The system uses FastAPI, SQLite, and Python standard libraries, making it transparent, inspectable, and reproducible. We evaluate APEX across three baselines and six scenarios using sample sizes 2-4x larger than initial experiments (N=20-40 per scenario). Results show that policy enforcement reduces total spending by 27.3% while maintaining 52.8% success rate for legitimate requests. Security mechanisms achieve 100% block rate for both replay attacks and invalid tokens with low latency overhead (19.6ms average). Multiple trial runs show low variance across scenarios, demonstrating high reproducibility with 95% confidence intervals. The primary contribution is a controlled agent-payment infrastructure and reference architecture that demonstrates how agentic access monetization can be adapted to fiat systems without discarding security and policy guarantees.

</details>


<div id='cond-mat.stat-mech'></div>

# cond-mat.stat-mech [[Back]](#toc)

### [493] [The topological gap at criticality: scaling exponent d + η, universality, and scope](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01484)
*Matthew Loftus*

Main category: cond-mat.stat-mech

TL;DR: This paper studies the topological gap in spin models and identifies critical correlations through finite-size scaling laws. It successfully applies these findings to the 2D Ising and 2D Potts q=3 models but identifies limitations for other cases like 2D Potts q=4 and 3D Ising.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the role of the topological gap in encoding critical correlations in spin models and develop a universal scaling theory.

Method: The study establishes finite-size scaling laws for the topological gap in spin models and compares the results with known theoretical predictions of critical exponents.

Result: The finite-size scaling theory was validated for 2D Ising and 2D Potts q=3 models, with excellent agreement between observed and theoretical critical exponents. However, it breaks down for cases with logarithmic corrections (e.g., 2D Potts q=4) or systems with first-order transitions, BKT, or percolation.

Conclusion: The scaling relation $α = d + η$ holds when corrections to scaling are algebraic but fails for logarithmic corrections or non-algebraic systems. This highlights the utility and limits of the presented framework.

Abstract: The topological gap $Δ= TP_{H_1}^{real} - TP_{H_1}^{shuf}$ -- the excess $H_1$ total persistence of the majority-spin alpha complex over a density-matched null -- encodes critical correlations in spin models. We establish finite-size scaling: $Δ(L,T) = A L^{d+η} G_-(L|t/T_c|)$, with $G_-(x) \sim (1+x/x_0)^{-(1+β/ν)}$. For 2D Ising, $α= 2.249 \pm 0.038$, matching $d+η= 9/4$ to $0.03σ$; the $G_-$ exponent $γ= 1.089 \pm 0.077$ is consistent with $1+β/ν= 9/8$ ($ΔR^2 < 10^{-5}$). For 2D Potts $q=3$ with $L$ up to 1024, $α= 2.272 \pm 0.024$ ($0.2σ$ from $d+η= 2.267$), with two-term corrections to scaling ($R^2 = 0.9999$). The $G_-$ exponent $γ= 1.114$ (68% CI $[1.053, 1.173]$) matches $1+β/ν= 17/15$. Scope boundaries: the law fails for 2D Potts $q=4$ ($α= 2.347 \pm 0.017$, $9.3σ$ from $d+η= 5/2$) where logarithmic corrections prevent convergence, and for raw 3D Ising ($4σ$ from $d+η$), but density normalization $Δ/|M|^{1/2}$ recovers $α= 3.06 \pm 0.04$ ($0.6σ$). The framework fails for first-order, BKT, and percolation. The criterion: $α= d+η$ holds when corrections to scaling are algebraic ($ω> 0$) but fails when logarithmic ($ω\to 0$).

</details>


<div id='physics.comp-ph'></div>

# physics.comp-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [494] [Experimental Design for Missing Physics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01231)
*Arno Strouwen,Sebastián Micluţa-Câmpeanu*

Main category: physics.comp-ph

TL;DR: The paper explores using sequential experimental design to better recover missing physics in process systems models using a combination of universal differential equations and symbolic regression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional methods struggle with incomplete model structures in process systems, and this paper aims to improve the discovery of missing physics using advanced machine learning techniques.

Method: It employs universal differential equations with neural networks to represent unknown physics and symbolic regression to make models interpretable. A sequential experimental design is introduced to gather quality data that optimally distinguishes between plausible model structures.

Result: The technique is successfully applied to discover missing physics in a bioreactor model, showcasing its utility in real-world applications.

Conclusion: Sequential experimental design, combined with universal differential equations and symbolic regression, effectively recovers accurate model structures in complex systems.

Abstract: For most process systems, knowledge of the model structure is incomplete. This missing physics must then be learned from experimental data. Recently, a combination of universal differential equations and symbolic regression has become a popular tool to discover these missing physics. Universal differential equations employ neural networks to represent missing parts of the model structure, and symbolic regression aims to make these neural networks interpretable. These machine learning techniques require high-quality data to successfully recover the true model structure. To gather such informative data, a sequential experimental design technique is developed which is based on optimally discriminating between the plausible model structures suggested by symbolic regression. This technique is then applied to discovering the missing physics of a bioreactor.

</details>


### [495] [Gradient estimators for parameter inference in discrete stochastic kinetic models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.02121)
*Ludwig Burger,Annalena Kofler,Lukas Heinrich,Ulrich Gerland*

Main category: physics.comp-ph

TL;DR: The paper evaluates the use of gradient estimators to enable parameter inference in stochastic kinetic models simulated by the Gillespie SSA, solving challenges posed by its non-differentiable operations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In stochastic kinetic models, parameter inference is critical but challenging due to the non-differentiability of stochastic simulation algorithms like the Gillespie algorithm.

Method: Three machine learning-based gradient estimators—the GS-ST estimator, the Score Function estimator, and the Alternative Path estimator—are applied to the Gillespie SSA and tested across representative systems with relaxation and oscillatory dynamics.

Result: The GS-ST estimator provides well-behaved gradient estimates but struggles with high variance in difficult parameter regimes, while other estimators prove more robust, offering better gradient stability in such cases.

Conclusion: Gradient-based parameter inference can be effectively integrated into the Gillespie SSA, with each gradient estimator offering unique benefits depending on the system's complexity and parameter regime.

Abstract: Stochastic kinetic models are ubiquitous in physics, yet inferring their parameters from experimental data remains challenging. In deterministic models, parameter inference often relies on gradients, as they can be obtained efficiently through automatic differentiation. However, these tools cannot be directly applied to stochastic simulation algorithms (SSA) such as the Gillespie algorithm, since sampling from a discrete set of reactions introduces non-differentiable operations. In this work, we adopt three gradient estimators from machine learning for the Gillespie SSA: the Gumbel-Softmax Straight-Through (GS-ST) estimator, the Score Function estimator, and the Alternative Path estimator. We compare the properties of all estimators in two representative systems exhibiting relaxation or oscillatory dynamics, where the latter requires gradient estimation of time-dependent objective functions. We find that the GS-ST estimator mostly yields well-behaved gradient estimates, but exhibits diverging variance in challenging parameter regimes, resulting in unsuccessful parameter inference. In these cases, the other estimators provide more robust, lower variance gradients. Our results demonstrate that gradient-based parameter inference can be integrated effectively with the Gillespie SSA, with different estimators offering complementary advantages.

</details>


<div id='cs.DL'></div>

# cs.DL [[Back]](#toc)

### [496] [Transforming OPACs into Intelligent Discovery Systems: An AI-Powered, Knowledge Graph-Driven Smart OPAC for Digital Libraries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01262)
*M. S. Rajeevan,B. Mini Devi*

Main category: cs.DL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a Smart OPAC framework using AI and knowledge graphs to enhance search and discovery in digital libraries, improving retrieval efficiency and contextual understanding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional OPACs struggle with efficient knowledge discovery due to increasing scholarly literature and outdated search techniques.

Method: The proposed framework uses AI, semantic search, knowledge graph-based techniques, semantic embeddings, and open scholarly data integration to improve search and navigation.

Result: The system shows quantitative improvements in retrieval efficiency, relevance, and reduction of information overload.

Conclusion: The framework modernizes OPACs and supports advanced research workflows, while future work aims for further personalization, user-centric evaluation, and dynamic updates.

Abstract: Traditional Online Public Access Catalogues (OPACs) are becoming less effective due to the rapid growth of scholarly literature. Conventional search methods, such as keyword indexing and Boolean queries, often fail to support efficient knowledge discovery. This paper proposes a Smart OPAC framework that transforms traditional OPACs into intelligent discovery systems using artificial intelligence and knowledge graph techniques. The framework enables semantic search, thematic filtering, and knowledge graph-based visualization to enhance user interaction and exploration. It integrates multiple open scholarly data sources and applies semantic embeddings to improve relevance and contextual understanding. The system supports exploratory search, semantic navigation, and refined result filtering based on user-defined themes. Quantitative evaluation demonstrates improvements in retrieval efficiency, relevance, and reduction of information overload. The proposed approach offers practical implications for modernizing digital library services and supports next-generation research workflows. Future work includes user-centric evaluation, personalization, and dynamic knowledge graph updates.

</details>


<div id='cs.IT'></div>

# cs.IT [[Back]](#toc)

### [497] [Best-Arm Identification with Noisy Actuation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.02255)
*Merve Karakas,Osama Hanna,Lin F. Yang,Christina Fragouli*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper explores identifying the best arm in a multi-armed bandit scenario using communication schemes over a discrete memoryless channel.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To solve the challenge of communicating arm commands from a central learner to a distributed agent effectively over a noisy channel in multi-armed bandit problems.

Method: The authors define arm identification strategies tailored to the agent's capabilities, leveraging the zero-error capacity properties of the DMC.

Result: Communication schemes were proposed, analyzed, and linked to the zero-error capacity concept, showcasing their applicability.

Conclusion: The paper offers optimized communication protocols for distributed decision-making in MAB settings, underpinned by DMC analysis.

Abstract: In this paper, we consider a multi-armed bandit (MAB) instance and study how to identify the best arm when arm commands are conveyed from a central learner to a distributed agent over a discrete memoryless channel (DMC). Depending on the agent capabilities, we provide communication schemes along with their analysis, which interestingly relate to the zero-error capacity of the underlying DMC.

</details>


<div id='cs.NI'></div>

# cs.NI [[Back]](#toc)

### [498] [Trustworthy AI-Driven Dynamic Hybrid RIS: Joint Optimization and Reward Poisoning-Resilient Control in Cognitive MISO Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01238)
*Deemah H. Tashman,Soumaya Cherkaoui*

Main category: cs.NI

TL;DR: This paper proposes an adaptive energy-aware hybrid reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) for cognitive radio networks (CRNs), optimizing its performance using deep reinforcement learning (DRL) and addressing adversarial attacks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address spectrum scarcity in wireless networks and improve SU performance in energy-constrained CRNs using a more dynamic and adaptive RIS approach.

Method: The paper employs a hybrid RIS that can switch between passive and active modes based on harvested energy availability. It uses the soft actor-critic (SAC) DRL method for joint optimization of transmit beamforming and RIS phase settings, while also introducing a defense mechanism against adversarial reward poisoning attacks.

Result: Numerical results show that the proposed SAC-based approach outperforms existing DRL techniques, and the dynamic hybrid RIS achieves better trade-offs between energy and throughput compared to static RIS architectures. Additionally, the defense mechanism effectively mitigates adversarial attacks.

Conclusion: The study enhances the practicality and security of RIS-assisted CRNs, providing a pathway for energy-efficient and resilient wireless system designs.

Abstract: Cognitive radio networks (CRNs) are a key mechanism for alleviating spectrum scarcity by enabling secondary users (SUs) to opportunistically access licensed frequency bands without harmful interference to primary users (PUs). To address unreliable direct SU links and energy constraints common in next-generation wireless networks, this work introduces an adaptive, energy-aware hybrid reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) for underlay multiple-input single-output (MISO) CRNs. Distinct from prior approaches relying on static RIS architectures, our proposed RIS dynamically alternates between passive and active operation modes in real time according to harvested energy availability. We also model our scenario under practical hardware impairments and cascaded fading channels. We formulate and solve a joint transmit beamforming and RIS phase optimization problem via the soft actor-critic (SAC) deep reinforcement learning (DRL) method, leveraging its robustness in continuous and highly dynamic environments. Notably, we conduct the first systematic study of reward poisoning attacks on DRL agents in RIS-enhanced CRNs, and propose a lightweight, real-time defense based on reward clipping and statistical anomaly filtering. Numerical results demonstrate that the SAC-based approach consistently outperforms established DRL baselines, and that the dynamic hybrid RIS strikes a superior trade-off between throughput and energy consumption compared to fully passive and fully active alternatives. We further show the effectiveness of our defense in maintaining SU performance even under adversarial conditions. Our results advance the practical and secure deployment of RIS-assisted CRNs, and highlight crucial design insights for energy-constrained wireless systems.

</details>


### [499] [ML-Enabled Open RAN: A Comprehensive Survey of Architectures, Challenges, and Opportunities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01239)
*Mira Chandra Kirana,Patatchona Keyela,Fatemeh Rostamian,Deemah H. Tashman,Soumaya Cherkaoui*

Main category: cs.NI

TL;DR: The paper surveys the role and potential of machine learning in Open Radio Access Networks (O-RAN), emphasizing its impact on network performance and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore how ML integration within O-RAN addresses challenges like spectrum management and resource allocation, while mapping directions for future research.

Method: Comprehensive literature review of existing ML applications in O-RAN.

Result: Provides insights into the transformative applications of ML in improving O-RAN functionality and highlights areas requiring further research.

Conclusion: ML offers promising advancements for O-RAN's interoperability and efficiency, guiding stakeholders in optimizing intelligent wireless networks.

Abstract: As wireless communication systems become more advanced, Open Radio Access Networks (O-RAN) stand out as a notable framework that promotes interoperability and cost-effectiveness. An examination of the progression of RAN architectures, as well as O-RAN's underlying principles, reveals the importance of machine learning (ML) in addressing various challenges, including spectrum management, resource allocation, and security. Hence, this survey provides a comprehensive overview of the integration of ML within O-RAN, highlighting its transformative potential in enhancing network performance and efficiency. This survey aims to describe the current status of ML applications in O-RAN while indicating possible directions for future research by analyzing existing literature. The findings aim to assist researchers and stakeholders in formulating optimal service strategies and advancing the understanding of intelligent wireless networks.

</details>


### [500] [Physics-Informed Transformer for Multi-Band Channel Frequency Response Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01944)
*Anatolij Zubow,Joana Angjo,Sigrid Dimce,Falko Dressler*

Main category: cs.NI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a physics-informed Transformer model to reconstruct wideband channel frequency responses (CFR) in multiband wireless systems even under interference blockage, outperforming traditional baselines in accuracy and robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the complexity of estimating wideband CFR in wireless systems with fragmented spectrum snapshots due to interference, especially in realistic and dynamic conditions.

Method: A complex Transformer model with factored self-attention is introduced, processing joint time-frequency grids and leveraging physics-informed loss. Training incorporates spectral fidelity, sparse channel impulse reconstruction, and velocity randomization for dynamic mobility scenarios.

Result: The model outperforms classical methods like last-observation-carry-forward and cubic-spline interpolation, achieving a PDP similarity score of $ρ\geq 0.82$, compared to $ρ\geq 0.62$ from baselines, and exhibits smooth performance across varying velocities.

Conclusion: The proposed method proves to be a robust and effective approach for fragmented spectrum reconstruction, presenting a significant advancement over traditional techniques, even in high-interference and mobility scenarios.

Abstract: Wideband channel frequency response (CFR) estimation is challenging in multi-band wireless systems, especially when one or more sub-bands are temporarily blocked by co-channel interference. We present a physics-informed complex Transformer that reconstructs the full wideband CFR from such fragmented, partially observed spectrum snapshots. The interference pattern in each sub-band is modeled as an independent two-state discrete-time Markov chain, capturing realistic bursty occupancy behavior. Our model operates on the joint time-frequency grid of $T$ snapshots and $F$ frequency bins and uses a factored self-attention mechanism that separately attends along both axes, reducing the computational complexity to $O(TF^2 + FT^2)$. Complex-valued inputs and outputs are processed through a holomorphic linear layer that preserves phase relationships. Training uses a composite physics-informed loss combining spectral fidelity, power delay profile (PDP) reconstruction, channel impulse response (CIR) sparsity, and temporal smoothness. Mobility effects are incorporated through per-sample velocity randomization, enabling generalization across different mobility regimes. Evaluation against three classical baselines, namely, last-observation-carry-forward, zero-fill, and cubic-spline interpolation, shows that our approach achieves the highest PDP similarity with respect to the ground truth, reaching $ρ\geq 0.82$ compared to $ρ\geq 0.62$ for the best baseline at interference occupancy levels up to 50%. Furthermore, the model degrades smoothly across the full velocity range, consistently outperforming all other baselines.

</details>


<div id='physics.chem-ph'></div>

# physics.chem-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [501] [VIANA: character Value-enhanced Intensity Assessment via domain-informed Neural Architecture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01365)
*Luana P. Queiroz,Icaro S. C. Bernardes,Ana M. Ribeiro,Bernardo M. Aguilera-Mercado,Idelfonso B. R. Nogueira*

Main category: physics.chem-ph

TL;DR: This paper introduces VIANA, a deep-learning framework that integrates molecular structure, semantic data, and dose-response logic to more accurately predict how humans perceive odor intensity, achieving significant improvements over traditional models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address the challenge of predicting odor intensity from molecular structures, as previous methods like GCNs fail to incorporate biological and perceptual contexts adequately.

Method: The paper employs a "tri-pillar" framework that integrates Graph Convolutional Networks for molecular structure, Principal Odor Map embeddings for semantic character values, and Hill's law for dose-response behaviors. Principal Component Analysis is used to distill semantic data for better relevance.

Result: The VIANA model achieved exceptional performance with a peak R^2 of 0.996 and a test MSE of 0.19, outperforming baseline models by capturing physical saturation, sensitivity thresholds, and semantic nuances effectively.

Conclusion: VIANA serves as a robust digital olfaction framework that bridges molecular structure and sensory perception, offering an advanced approach to simulate human olfactory experiences.

Abstract: Predicting the perceived intensity of odorants remains a fundamental challenge in sensory science due to the complex, non-linear behavior of their response, as well as the difficulty in correlating molecular structure with human perception. While traditional deep learning models, such as Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs), excel at capturing molecular topology, they often fail to account for the biological and perceptual context of olfaction. This study introduces VIANA, a novel "tri-pillar" framework that integrates structural graph theory, character value embeddings, and phenomenological behavior. This methodology systematically evaluates knowledge transfer across three distinct domains: molecular structure via GCNs, semantic odor character values via Principal Odor Map (POM) embeddings, and biological dose-response logic via Hill's law. We demonstrate that knowledge transfer is not inherently positive; rather, a balance must be maintained in the volume of information provided to the model. While raw semantic data led to "information overload" in domain-informed models, applying Principal Component Analysis (PCA) to distill the 95% most impactful semantic variance yielded a superior "signal distillation" effect. Results indicate that the synthesis of these three knowledge transfer pillars significantly outperforms baseline structural models, with VIANA achieving a peak R^2 of 0.996 and a test Mean Squared Error (MSE) of 0.19. In this context, VIANA successfully captures the physical ceiling of saturation, the sensitivity of detection thresholds, and the nuance of odor character value expression, providing a domain grounded simulation of the human olfactory experience. This research provides a robust framework for digital olfaction, effectively bridging the gap between molecular informatics and sensory perception.

</details>


<div id='stat.AP'></div>

# stat.AP [[Back]](#toc)

### [502] [Country-wide, high-resolution monitoring of forest browning with Sentinel-2](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.02074)
*Samantha Biegel,David Brüggemann,Francesco Grossi,Michele Volpi,Konrad Schindler,Benjamin D. Stocker*

Main category: stat.AP

TL;DR: This study presents a scalable approach using Sentinel-2 data to monitor forest greenness anomalies across Switzerland, enabling better insights into disturbances.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Forest health is being negatively impacted by both natural and human-induced disturbances, necessitating scalable monitoring methods for conservation.

Method: They use a quantile model leveraging Sentinel-2 NDVI data, ecological/topographical context, and seasonal vegetation cycles to detect anomalies.

Result: The model explains 65% of seasonal NDVI cycle variations and shows high reliability in identifying spatial patterns and disturbance types.

Conclusion: This approach enables accurate country-wide mapping of forest changes, benefiting conservation strategies and disturbance detection.

Abstract: Natural and anthropogenic disturbances are impacting the health of forests worldwide. Monitoring forest disturbances at scale is important to inform conservation efforts. Here, we present a scalable approach for country-wide mapping of forest greenness anomalies at the 10 m resolution of Sentinel-2. Using relevant ecological and topographical context and an established representation of the vegetation cycle, we learn a predictive quantile model of the normalised difference vegetation index (NDVI) derived from Sentinel-2 data. The resulting expected seasonal cycles are used to detect NDVI anomalies across Switzerland between April 2017 and August 2025. Goodness-of-fit evaluations show that the conditional model explains 65% of the observed variations in the median seasonal cycle. The model consistently benefits from the local context information, particularly during the green-up period. The approach produces coherent spatial anomaly patterns and enables country-wide quantification of forest browning. Case studies with independent reference data from known events illustrate that the model reliably detects different types of disturbances.

</details>


<div id='econ.TH'></div>

# econ.TH [[Back]](#toc)

### [503] [All Substitution Is Local](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01443)
*Nidhish Shah,Shaurjya Mandal,Asfandyar Azhar*

Main category: econ.TH

TL;DR: The paper examines information interactions for Bayesian decision-making. It identifies complement and substitute forces in information dynamics and establishes a "localization principle" governing them.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how the combination of multiple information sources influences Bayesian decision-making and identify the underlying dynamics between complementary and substitutive interactions.

Method: A theoretical study integrating Bayesian analysis for decision-making with formal proofs and codification in Lean 4 to examine correlated information sources.

Result: Complementary interactions occur when information sources increase each other's usefulness. Substitutive interactions, however, arise when crossing decision boundaries. Beyond boundaries, mutual cooperation of information is consistent.

Conclusion: Information sources tend to complement each other unless decisions change through boundary-crossing observation. Substitution exists locally, proving information interplay is largely cooperative unless on decision-altering borders.

Abstract: When does consulting one information source raise the value of another, and when does it diminish it? We study this question for Bayesian decision-makers facing finite actions. The interaction decomposes into two opposing forces: a complement force, measuring how one source moves beliefs to where the other becomes more useful, and a substitute force, measuring how much the current decision is resolved. Their balance obeys a localization principle: substitution requires an observation to cross a decision boundary, though crossing alone does not guarantee it. Whenever posteriors remain inside the current decision region, the substitute force vanishes, and sources are guaranteed to complement each other, even when one source cannot, on its own, change the decision. The results hold for arbitrarily correlated sources and are formalized in Lean 4. Substitution is confined to the thin boundaries where decisions change. Everywhere else, information cooperates. Code and proofs: https://github.com/nidhishs/all-substitution-is-local.

</details>


<div id='q-fin.MF'></div>

# q-fin.MF [[Back]](#toc)

### [504] [Reinforcement Learning for Speculative Trading under Exploratory Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.02035)
*Yun Zhao,Alex S. L. Tse,Harry Zheng*

Main category: q-fin.MF

TL;DR: This paper analyzes a speculative trading problem using exploratory reinforcement learning, reformulates it into a relaxed optimization framework, and develops a corresponding RL algorithm applied to a pairs-trading scenario.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation lies in addressing complex speculative trading scenarios by utilizing reinforcement learning methods, providing a more flexible and effective framework to identify optimal entry and exit times in trading.

Method: The authors model entry and exit times as a sequential optimal stopping problem, relaxed through Cox processes with non-randomized intensity controls. The exploratory RL framework is employed with Shannon entropy regularization, leading to a system of exploratory HJB equations and closed-form optimal Gibbs distributions.

Result: They establish error estimates, convergence guarantees, and develop an RL algorithm applied to pairs-trading. The algorithm's performance demonstrates the practical application of the proposed method.

Conclusion: The paper successfully integrates exploratory RL with speculative trading, providing theoretical justifications and practical implementation in a pairs-trading setup, marking an advancement in financial applications of RL.

Abstract: We study a speculative trading problem within the exploratory reinforcement learning (RL) framework of Wang et al. [2020]. The problem is formulated as a sequential optimal stopping problem over entry and exit times under general utility function and price process. We first consider a relaxed version of the problem in which the stopping times are modeled by the jump times of Cox processes driven by bounded, non-randomized intensity controls. Under the exploratory formulation, the agent's randomized control is characterized via the probability measure over the jump intensities, and their objective function is regularized by Shannon's differential entropy. This yields a system of the exploratory HJB equations and Gibbs distributions in closed-form as the optimal policy. Error estimates and convergence of the RL objective to the value function of the original problem are established. Finally, an RL algorithm is designed, and its implementation is showcased in a pairs-trading application.

</details>


<div id='math.PR'></div>

# math.PR [[Back]](#toc)

### [505] [Homogenized Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01978)
*Hugo Koubbi,Borjan Geshkovski,Philippe Rigollet*

Main category: math.PR

TL;DR: This paper analyzes a deep multi-head self-attention model, representing it as a particle system and identifying its dynamics under different scaling regimes, revealing trade-offs that influence clustering behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to understand the dynamics of deep multi-head self-attention models, especially in terms of their interaction structure, homogenized limits, and potential representation collapse during initialization.

Method: The study models the residual stream as a discrete-time particle system on the unit sphere, and applies mathematical analysis to derive homogenized limit behaviors under specific scalings of parameters.

Result: The research derives deterministic and stochastic homogenized limits, connects certain scalings to a Fokker–Planck equation, and identifies scenarios where dimensionality, context length, and temperature influence clustering and collapse.

Conclusion: The paper provides mathematical insights into initialization properties of self-attention models and outlines trade-offs that impact token representations in these systems.

Abstract: We study a random model of deep multi-head self-attention in which the weights are resampled independently across layers and heads, as at initialization of training. Viewing depth as a time variable, the residual stream defines a discrete-time interacting particle system on the unit sphere. We prove that, under suitable joint scalings of the depth, the residual step size, and the number of heads, this dynamics admits a nontrivial homogenized limit. Depending on the scaling, the limit is either deterministic or stochastic with common noise; in the mean-field regime, the latter leads to a stochastic nonlinear Fokker--Planck equation for the conditional law of a representative token. In the Gaussian setting, the limiting drift vanishes, making the homogenized dynamics explicit enough to study representation collapse. This yields quantitative trade-offs between dimension, context length, and temperature, and identifies regimes in which clustering can be mitigated.

</details>


<div id='eess.SP'></div>

# eess.SP [[Back]](#toc)

### [506] [Evaluation of gNB Monostatic Sensing for UAV Use Case](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.02205)
*Steve Blandino,Neeraj Varshney,Jian Wang,Jack Chuang,Camillo Gentile,Nada Golmie*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper evaluates 5G NR base station-based monostatic sensing for UAV use cases, achieving high detection probability and low localization errors, and releases a simulator for reproducibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the need for reproducible end-to-end studies in ISAC while leveraging the standardization efforts in 3GPP Release 19.

Method: An end-to-end processing chain for multi-target detection and 3D localization is implemented, using a 5G NR CP-OFDM waveform and positioning reference signals under UMa-AV scenario assumptions.

Result: The study achieved over 70% detection probability with under 5% false alarm rate and localization errors in the range of a few meters, with 90th-percentile errors of 4m (vertical) and 6m (horizontal).

Conclusion: The results demonstrate the feasibility of 5G NR-based monostatic sensing for UAV applications, and the release of the 5GNRad simulator supports reproducibility and further research.

Abstract: 3GPP Release 19 has initiated the standardization of integrated sensing and communications (ISAC), including a channel model for monostatic sensing, evaluation scenarios, and performance assessment methodologies. These common assumptions provide an important basis for ISAC evaluation, but reproducible end-to-end studies still require a transparent sensing implementation. This paper evaluates 5G New Radio (NR) base station (gNB)-based monostatic sensing for the Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) use case using a 5G NR downlink Cyclic Prefix-Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (CP-OFDM) waveform and positioning reference signals (PRS), following 3GPP Urban Macro-Aerial Vehicle (UMa-AV) scenario assumptions. We present an end-to-end processing chain for multi-target detection and 3D localization, achieving more than 70% detection probability with less than 5% false alarm rate, in the considered scenario. For correctly detected targets, localization errors are on the order of a few meters, with a 90th-percentile error of 4m and 6m in the vertical and horizontal directions, respectively. To support reproducible baseline studies and further research, we release the simulator 5GNRad, which reproduces our evaluation

</details>


### [507] [Interpretable Battery Aging without Extra Tests via Neural-Assisted Physics-based Modelling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01229)
*Yuan Qiu,Wei Li,Wei Zhang,Yi Zhou,Fang Liu,Jianbiao Wang,Zhi Wei Seh*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper presents IBAM, a neural-assisted physics-based framework for creating interpretable 2-D aging fingerprints for batteries, which provides deeper insights for battery management compared to traditional health scalar metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional battery health metrics like SoH are limited in providing deep interpretability, making it difficult to optimize battery operation due to varying degradation behaviors.

Method: The proposed IBAM framework integrates a physics-based battery model, derived from a fractional-order equivalent circuit model, with a two-stage least-squares technique. It combines this with a bidirectional gated recurrent unit for tracking SoH through multi-channel voltage features.

Result: IBAM achieves high fidelity in modeling battery physics across varying lifespans and, through its interpretable 2-D fingerprinting, elucidates distinct degradation mechanisms and patterns for batteries with different lifespans.

Conclusion: IBAM enhances battery health assessment by introducing interpretable fingerprints that offer actionable insights into degradation mechanisms, paving the way for better battery management and control.

Abstract: State of health (SoH) is widely used for battery management, but it is a single scalar and offers limited interpretability. Two batteries with similar SoH can exhibit very different degradation behaviors and the lack of interpretability hinders optimal battery operation. In this paper, we propose IBAM for interpretable battery aging modelling with a neural-assisted physics-based framework. IBAM outputs a 2-D aging fingerprint without extra diagnostic tests and uses only routine logs from the battery management system. The fingerprint offers great interpretability by capturing a battery's curve-wide polarization voltage loss and the tail loss near the end-of-discharge. IBAM first creates a physics-based battery model based on a fractional-order equivalent circuit model, and then extracts per-cycle fingerprints from the model using a two-stage least-squares method. IBAM further anchors fingerprints on the SoH axis with physics-guided regression, where the per-cycle SoH is estimated via a bidirectional gated recurrent unit with customized multi-channel voltage features. Across batteries with short-, medium-, and long-lifespans, IBAM consistently yields the best physics model fidelity at different aging stages, and provides clear interpretations of degradation mechanisms and fingerprint patterns about batteries of different lifespans. The resulting fingerprints support interpretable battery health assessment and can inform battery control choices.

</details>
